name: Applied Anatomy & Physiology
description: Structure and function of the musculoskeletal, cardiorespiratory and neuromuscular systems during physical activity

subskills.name: Effects of Exercise on Body Systems

commonMisconceptions: Ligaments connect muscle to bone (ligaments connect bone to bone; tendons connect muscle to bone)
The heart is one big pump (it has four chambers with separate pulmonary and systemic circuits)
Muscles can push as well as pull (muscles can only contract and pull; they work in antagonistic pairs)

name: Movement Analysis
description: Lever systems, planes and axes of movement, and mechanical advantage in sporting actions
commonMisconceptions: All levers in the body give mechanical advantage (most are third class levers, which favour speed and range of movement over force)
Isometric means the muscle shortens (isometric contraction involves no change in muscle length)

name: Physical Training
description: Components of fitness, principles of training, training methods and prevention of injury
commonMisconceptions: Fitness and health are the same thing (fitness is the ability to meet the demands of the environment; health is a state of complete physical, mental and social well-being)
Stretching before exercise prevents all injuries (warm-up raises body temperature and heart rate; stretching alone is not sufficient)
Progressive overload means always lifting heavier weights (it can also involve increasing reps, sets, duration or frequency)

name: Sports Psychology
description: Mental preparation, motivation, arousal, personality and aggression in sport
commonMisconceptions: Higher arousal always improves performance (the inverted-U theory shows performance declines when arousal is too high)
Aggression in sport is always negative (assertion and channelled aggression can be positive and within the rules)
Intrinsic and extrinsic motivation cannot coexist (performers are often driven by a combination of both)

name: Socio-Cultural Influences
description: Social, cultural and ethical factors affecting participation and performance in physical activity and sport
commonMisconceptions: The media only has positive effects on sport (it can also create negative pressure, bias coverage and promote unhealthy body images)
Performance-enhancing drugs only affect the individual athlete (they undermine fair competition and influence younger athletes)
Commercialisation benefits all sports equally (minority and women's sports often receive far less funding and coverage)

name: Health, Fitness & Well-being
description: The relationship between health, fitness and well-being, including sedentary lifestyles and diet

subskills.name: Diet and Its Impact on Health

commonMisconceptions: Being thin means you are fit and healthy (health encompasses physical, mental and social well-being, not just body weight)
A sedentary lifestyle only affects physical health (it also impacts mental health and social well-being)
Exercise alone is enough for good health (diet, sleep and mental health are equally important)

name: Use of Data
description: Collecting, presenting and analysing data to evaluate performance in physical activity and sport
commonMisconceptions: Quantitative data is always more useful than qualitative data (both have value; qualitative data provides context that numbers cannot)
A single fitness test result tells you everything about a performer (multiple tests and repeated measures give a more accurate picture)
Normative data tables apply equally to everyone regardless of age or sex (tables are specific to population groups)

name: Biomechanics
description: Forces, motion and the application of Newton's laws to sporting performance
commonMisconceptions: Heavier objects always fall faster (in the absence of air resistance, all objects accelerate at the same rate due to gravity)
A ball in flight has a forward force acting on it (once released, only gravity and air resistance act on it; there is no forward propelling force)
Newton's third law means forces cancel out (the action and reaction act on different objects, so they do not cancel)

name: Nutrition
description: The role of nutrients, hydration and dietary planning for physical activity and sport
commonMisconceptions: All fats are bad for you (unsaturated fats are essential for insulation, organ protection and energy)
Protein supplements are necessary to build muscle (a balanced diet usually provides sufficient protein)
Drinking water only matters during exercise (hydration before, during and after activity is important)

name: Practical Performance
description: Application of skills, tactics and strategies in physical activity and sport, including analysis and evaluation
commonMisconceptions: Natural talent is more important than practice (deliberate practice and effective training are key to improvement)
Tactics are only relevant in team sports (individual sports also require strategic thinking and game plans)
Analysis of performance is only about watching video replays (it includes self-reflection, peer observation and data analysis)
